beats.activity: Wake up, check on sourdough starter, make coffee and toast.
beats.reasoning: Viktor wakes early on Tuesday to check on the starter he began yesterday evening; morning ritual includes coffee and reviewing the day ahead while the rain patters outside.

beats.activity: Drive south to Tukwila site, taking I-5 to avoid SR-520 eastbound closure.
beats.reasoning: SR-520 eastbound is closed 9am–noon for emergency repair; Viktor departs early and routes via I-5 to ensure he arrives before the bottleneck. Rain is steady but manageable for driving.

beats.activity: On-site project management: inspect framing progress, coordinate crews, check measurements and weather impacts.
beats.reasoning: Viktor spends the morning shift overseeing the framing work; the rain from 7am–2pm doesn't halt the schedule, but he stays focused on the timeline and budget. Avoids the SR-520 closure by being already at the site.

beats.activity: Lunch at the site trailer: sandwich and coffee while reviewing crew timesheets.
beats.reasoning: Viktor eats on-site as he does most days, keeping overhead low and staying connected to the work. Rain is still falling; indoor meal is practical and fits his workaholic style.

beats.activity: Continue on-site oversight: afternoon inspections, troubleshoot a framing detail, coordinate with subcontractors.
beats.reasoning: The afternoon shift runs through to late afternoon. Rain is clearing by 2pm, but Viktor remains focused on the schedule. He's meticulous and values staying ahead of delays.

beats.activity: Drive back north to Seattle via I-5, navigating post-rain traffic.
beats.reasoning: SR-520 closure has ended by now; Viktor still takes I-5 to be safe. The drive is steady but longer than usual due to rain-dampened roads and commute traffic.

beats.activity: Stop at QFC to pick up supplies: bread flour, eggs, and a rotisserie chicken for dinner.
beats.reasoning: Viktor stops at the neighborhood QFC on the way home; he's methodical about sourcing good ingredients for his sourdough work and tonight's meal. Efficiency matters—in and out in 40 minutes.

beats.activity: Prepare dinner: warm the rotisserie chicken, make a simple salad, pour a beer.
beats.reasoning: Viktor keeps dinner simple on a work night; he's tired but satisfied with the day's progress on the Tukwila site. The quiet meal at home aligns with his preference for low-key evenings.

beats.activity: Stretch and fold the sourdough starter, refresh it, set up for bulk fermentation overnight.; Review daily expense spreadsheet, check tomorrow's weather forecast, read a chapter of his book.
beats.reasoning: Viktor's sourdough project is both hobby and meditation; he takes time to nurture the dough and prepare it for an overnight bulk fermentation. This is his wind-down ritual—hands-on, creative, and grounding after a long workday.

beats.activity: Sleep, with sourdough bulk-fermenting on the counter nearby.
beats.reasoning: Viktor settles into bed after a solid, productive day. The dough rests quietly in its container while he sleeps; he'll check it first thing Wednesday morning. A full 7h 45m of sleep sets him up well for another early start.

diary: Woke to the sourdough starter bubbling away—always a good sign. The rain was relentless this morning, so I ditched SR-520 and took I-5 down to Tukwila. Smart call; the bridge closure would've made it a nightmare. Site looked decent despite the weather. We're making real progress on the framing, though there's always something—today it was a detail that didn't quite line up. Spent most of the afternoon coordinating with the subs to get it sorted. The drive back was slow and damp but manageable. Grabbed supplies at QFC on the way home, and by evening I had a simple dinner ready and my hands back in the dough. There's something grounding about stretch-and-folding after a long day managing a hundred moving parts. The bulk fermentation is set for tomorrow. Quiet night, really.
thought_process: The weather pushed me to reroute early—I've learned over the years that getting ahead of traffic snarls saves hours of frustration. The site work was steady; that framing detail nagged at me because precision matters, and I wanted it resolved before end of day rather than becoming tomorrow's crisis. By afternoon, once the rain cleared and the commute felt manageable, my mood lifted. I was thinking about the sourdough the whole time I was away—not anxiously, just aware it needed attention. Baking has become my counterweight to the site chaos; it's tactile and slow and entirely within my control. Reviewing the expense spreadsheet felt routine, almost meditative. I ended the day feeling balanced—work handled, home tended to, fermentation started. That's a good Tuesday.
world_event_prompt: Grey, drizzly Tuesday with steady rain from 7am to 2pm, high of 58F. SR-520 bridge closed eastbound 9am-noon for emergency repair, snarling cross-lake traffic.
